title: Security First Act
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Counterterrorism and Intelligence.
plain_summary: The Security First Act gives money to help secure the US border with Mexico from 2025 to 2028. The bill uses money taken from people who try to cross the border illegally with unreported cash to fund a program called Operation Stonegarden, which helps local police work on border security. The bill also requires the Department of Homeland Security to write reports about their hiring practices, whether certain gangs should be called terrorist groups, and what technology is needed to make the border more secure.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Counterterrorism and Intelligence.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Security First Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. Since fiscal year twenty twenty one, more than eight point seven two million people have been encountered at the Southwest border. Since fiscal year twenty twenty one, roughly two million known people called gotaways have avoided United States Border Patrol. Since fiscal year twenty twenty one, there were three hundred ninety five encounters at our borders with people on the Terrorist Screening Dataset, which is also known as the terrorist watchlist. Since fiscal year twenty twenty one, nine thousand two hundred fifty four pounds of fentanyl have been seized between ports of entry nationwide. In twenty twenty three, there were one hundred five thousand seven opioid deaths, with seventy two thousand seven hundred seventy six deaths involving synthetic opioids like fentanyl. Mexican cartels and transnational criminal organizations have been linked to committing severe crimes including homicides, rape, sexual assault, and kidnappings. They significantly influence drug trafficking and human trafficking across the Southwest border, organizing and profiting from these illegal operations. In twenty twenty four, U.S. Border Patrol sent out multiple alerts to personnel warning of Mexican cartel members being permitted to shoot at Border Patrol Agents and engage in retaliatory shootings, which is a direct threat to homeland security. In twenty twenty four, Homeland Security Investigations sent out an alert to personnel that Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua had been given the green light to fire on or attack law enforcement in Colorado, which is a direct threat to homeland security. State and local law enforcement continue to assume a larger and critical role helping federal law enforcement in border security operations with limited resources. Federal, State, and local law enforcement need the resources necessary to secure U.S. borders and protect the homeland.

sections.title: Section 3  Operation Stonegarden Appropriations and Trust Fund
sections.text: The government is authorized to spend one hundred ten million dollars for each of fiscal years twenty twenty five through twenty twenty eight for the Operation Stonegarden grant program. Also, at least thirty six million six hundred sixty six thousand six hundred sixty six dollars for each of fiscal years twenty twenty five through twenty twenty eight will be used to buy technology and equipment, including communications equipment, sensors, and drone technology. A trust fund called the Operation Stonegarden Trust Fund will be created in the Treasury of the United States. This fund will consist of amounts transferred to the Trust Fund. The Secretary of the Treasury will transfer money to the Trust Fund from the general fund of the Treasury. For fiscal year twenty twenty five and each fiscal year after that until twenty twenty eight, the amount transferred will equal the amount received into the general fund during that fiscal year from unreported monetary instruments seized by U.S. Customs and Border Protection from individuals crossing the United States and Mexico border. Money in the Trust Fund will be made available to the Secretary of Homeland Security, without needing additional approval, to fund the Operation Stonegarden grant program. The Secretary may only spend funds made available from the Trust Fund to carry out this activity. A monetary instrument means coin or currency of the United States or of any other country, travelers checks in any form, negotiable instruments including checks promissory notes and money orders in bearer form endorsed without restriction made out to a fake payee or otherwise in such form that title passes upon delivery, incomplete instruments including checks promissory notes and money orders that are signed but on which the name of the payee has been omitted, and securities or stock in bearer form or otherwise in such form that title passes upon delivery. A monetary instrument does not include checks or money orders made payable to the order of a named person which have not been endorsed or which bear restrictive endorsements, warehouse receipts, or bills of lading.

sections.title: Section 4  Foreign Terrorist Organization Designations
sections.text: Not later than sixty days after this law is enacted, the Secretary of State must submit a report to the appropriate congressional committees about whether Mexican drug cartels and criminal gangs meet the criteria for designation as foreign terrorist organizations. The Mexican drug cartels described are the Jalisco New Generation Cartel, Sinaloa Cartel, Juarez Cartel, Tijuana Cartel, Gulf Cartel, and Los Zetas. The criminal gang described is the Tren De Aragua. The appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Foreign Affairs and the Committee on Homeland Security of the House of Representatives and the Committee on Foreign Relations and the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs of the Senate. Foreign terrorist organization has the meaning given in section two hundred nineteen of the Immigration and Nationality Act. Secretary means the Secretary of State.

sections.title: Section 5  Southern Border Technology Needs Analysis and Updates
sections.text: Not later than one year after this law is enacted, the Secretary of Homeland Security must submit a technology needs analysis for border security technology along the Southwest border to the appropriate congressional committees. The analysis must include an assessment of the technology needs and gaps along the Southwest border to prevent terrorists and instruments of terror from entering the United States, to combat and reduce cross border criminal activity including the transport of illegal goods such as illicit drugs and human smuggling and human trafficking, and to facilitate the flow of legal trade across the Southwest border. The analysis must also assess recent technological advancements in manned aircraft sensor communication and common operating picture technology, unmanned aerial systems and related technology including counter unmanned aerial system technology, surveillance technology including mobile surveillance vehicles associated electronics including cameras sensor technology and radar tower based surveillance technology advanced unattended surveillance sensors and deployable lighter than air ground surveillance equipment, nonintrusive inspection technology including non x ray devices utilizing muon tomography and other advanced detection technology, tunnel detection technology, and communications equipment including radios long term evolution broadband and miniature satellites. The analysis must cover any other technological advancements that the Secretary determines to be critical to the Department's mission along the Southwest border. It must assess whether the use of these technological advances will improve border security, improve the capability of the Department to accomplish its mission along the Southwest border, reduce technology gaps along the Southwest border, and enhance the safety of any officer or agent of the Department or any other Federal agency. The analysis must also cover the Department's ongoing border security technology development efforts including efforts by U.S. Customs and Border Protection the Science and Technology Directorate and the technology assessment office of any other operational component. It must address the technology needs for improving border security such as information technology or other computer or computing systems data capture biometrics cloud storage and intelligence data sharing capabilities among agencies within the Department. The analysis must include any other technological needs or factors including border security infrastructure such as physical barriers or dual purpose infrastructure that the Secretary determines should be considered. Finally, it must cover currently deployed technology or new technology that would improve the Department's ability to reasonably achieve operational control and situational awareness along the Southwest border and to collect metrics for securing the border at and between ports of entry. Not later than two years after the submission of the analysis and every two years after that for the following four years, the Secretary must submit an update to the analysis to the appropriate congressional committees. Each update must include a plan for utilizing the resources of the Department to meet the border security technology needs and gaps identified including developing or acquiring technologies not currently in use by the Department that would allow the Department to bridge existing border technology gaps along the Southwest border. In compiling the technology needs analysis and updates, the Secretary must consider and examine technology that is deployed and is sufficient for the Department's use along the Southwest border, technology that is deployed but is insufficient for the Department's use along the Southwest border, technology that is not deployed but is necessary for the Department's use along the Southwest border, current formal departmental requirements documentation examining current border security threats and challenges faced by any component of the Department, trends and forecasts regarding migration across the Southwest border, the impact on projected staffing and deployment needs for the Department including staffing needs that may be fulfilled through the use of technology, the needs and challenges faced by employees of the Department who are deployed along the Southwest border, the need to improve cooperation among Federal State Tribal local and Mexican law enforcement entities to enhance security along the Southwest border, the privacy implications of existing technology and the acquisition and deployment of new technologies and supporting infrastructure with an emphasis on how privacy risks might be mitigated through the use of technology training and policy, the impact of any ongoing public health emergency that impacts Department operations along the Southwest border, and the ability of and the needs for the Department to assist with search and rescue efforts for individuals or groups that may be in physical danger or in need of medical assistance. To the extent possible, the Secretary must submit the technology needs analysis and updates in unclassified form but may submit such documents or portions of such documents in classified form if the Secretary determines that such action is appropriate. The appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs of the Senate and the Committee on Homeland Security of the House of Representatives. Department means the Department of Homeland Security. Secretary means the Secretary of Homeland Security. Southwest border means the international land border between the United States and Mexico including the ports of entry along such border.

sections.title: Section 6  Report Relating to Hiring Practices of the Department from 2018 to 2024
sections.text: Not later than one hundred twenty days after this law is enacted, the Secretary of Homeland Security must submit to the appropriate congressional committees a report relating to the hiring practices of the Department. The report must include information relating to the recruitment practices of the Department from twenty eighteen to twenty twenty four and recommendations with respect to improving the operational capacity of the Department workforce.
